speaker: interviewer
text: --Is May 2nd, 1995. Doing an interview with Eva Shane. Tape three. Last, we spoke about your trip back from the battle, on your way back to Antwerp, to go back home in Belgium with your mother and your brother. Can you tell me how you found Antwerp when you arrived? And we're still in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was still in 1940. We came back, I think, in June, a month later. And life was very different. It was like coming back to different city or a different country. There was no question of going back to school for a Jewish child. German, German soldiers were everywhere. The country was occupied officially. Everything was under German rule. And then people were very afraid. They were afraid to talk. They were afraid to-- and, and Jewish people especially. We had this feel that something was brewing, something was going to happen. And it didn't start right away, but very gradually. Like, one day, they came with a rule that-- for curfew, Jewish people were not allowed outside after six o'clock. My mother told us, look. We're not-- we're not going to register. My father told us that already in his letter. By, by the time we had-- later, we got contact with my father. He started writing, and we knew where he was. And he says, don't register as Jew. If you do, it's like committing suicide. You are dead. If you don't, maybe they'll find out, and, and you'll get-- and you'll get deported anyways. But you still have a slight chance to escape. So it's better not to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember visiting Jewish friends after six. And they opened the door, and they were horrified. They said, how can you be in the street? You're going to get arrested. You're going to get killed. And my mother said, I ignore it. I'm, I'm not going to go for that. She take a-- she took a chance. She took a chance. Can you explain what the registration meant? And where did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to report to the police, or to a certain-- I think it was usually police who worked with the German, you know, for the Germans. And you had to register there as, as a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were issued papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they've got a J on your-- on your paper, you know, meaning Jew, and a, a Jewish star. And then the next step, of course, was to wear the Jewish star. Then they start-- and then we knew it was time to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they started the order for-- to wear the Jewish star, it's like a target, you know? I mean, what, what else does it mean? I mean, you put two and two together. Then my father wrote us, leave. Don't care, don't care. Because we-- what are we going to do? We had a big house. What do we do with all our-- with all our furniture, with all our things? We can't just abandon it. My father said, abandon it. Leave it. Your life is more important. So we had neighbors across the street, and he was a retired businessman. He used to be in the wine business. And he had a huge warehouse where he kept his wine. And he told us, look, I have a lot of room in the warehouse. You can-- you can put your stuff there, free of charge. And so that was also wonderful. That was the first step, really, that saved us. So otherwise, maybe we would have stayed because of our furniture, you know? Many people lost their lives because they couldn't separate themselves from, from their belongings. So we had our furniture moved to the warehouse. Of course, there were a lot of people still working there. And some of-- some of the cases were opened, and a lot of stuff was missing later. But still, most of it was still there when we came back. And it was in good condition. This man that helped you, was he a Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Yes. Not only that, but he wrote-- we stayed in correspondence with him during the war. And in one of his first letters, he mentioned that there were two men had came a few days after we left, rang our bell. And then they came to the neighbors, and they crossed the street, and they asked him. They rang his bell, and they asked him if he knew where we went. And he said he didn't know. He thinks-- he thought we went to Switzerland. Of course, he knew where we were, but he didn't tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we kept thinking all these-- all this time, all these years, oh, we-- you know, who could that have been? Who could that have been? A relative? A friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we came back in Antwerp, to Antwerp, and my-- we went to the police station to register. You always had to register. And the, the person in charge, he says, oh, by the way, I don't know if you're aware of this. He came out-- he came with a file. He said, three, I think, two or three days after we left, the Gestapo came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they also came to the police, and they, they wanted to know where we end up. And he even mentioned they wore black leather coats. Ever since, I have this aversion, you know? Because Gestapo, they wore a lot of black leather coats. That was a Gestapo. Then we found out that was the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to the police station, and they ripped our-- and, and they wrote down for, for reason of arrest, they wanted-- they wanted us for reason of arrest. That was only a few days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I don't know. Why they came-- you know, they never needed [INAUDIBLE]. One of the neighbors had tried to turn us in. We had one neighbor who was a Nazi. And that was in the report, that supposedly, we were making signs from our attic to the Allied planes. He saw a light going on and off. We were doing-- we were making secret signs to the-- to the Ally. That was in the report.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe that's the reason they came to arrest us. Who knows? But, you know, a few days. I mean, it-- that was like a miracle. And luckily, he didn't tell them. He had a-- by instinct, he didn't tell them. He didn't tell anybody where we were. If he had told, who knows? When you arrived back in Antwerp, and then you registered, how-- your mom registered you, and your brother, and your mother as--

speaker: interviewee
text: As, you know, regular. Later-- that, that was later that we-- you had to register as Jew. And she says, no. You know, she ignored it. So, and-- of course, with our name, it was a very dangerous thing to do. Because our name being Cohn, was considered by Germans the most Jewish name of all. Even more so than Levy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was the worst name to have. So we took a big chance. You know, Cohn and not Jewish? You know, didn't make sense. But we took a chance. For that short time that you were in Antwerp, before you went on your journey, how long were you there when you got back? And then what was your life like during those days? It was-- it was pretty hectic, and it was pretty bad. There was no food already. There was-- except for black market. Very little food. So people talked a lot about food. You know, you always talk about what you don't have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And social life was practically down to zero because Jews were afraid to go in the street. They were afraid to be recognized as Jews, to be arrested. People got arrested constantly, and right in the street. Somebody goes shopping-- oh, shopping. There was nothing to buy. Somebody goes and never comes back. You know, that was common. And you never knew what happened. You get arrested constantly in the street. Show me your paper, show me your identity card. You know, suddenly, there was like, a soldier in front of you. Identity card. And they would cut off a block, usually, or a few blocks. You know, so people couldn't escape. And then everybody in that section, they would check the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if anything was suspicious, right away, you disappeared into a truck. And that was it. That was life, you know? That was everyday life. Constantly. So people were afraid to go in the street, and they tried to avoid, as much as possible, visit each other, or whatever. And, and if we did meet with friends, it was always the conversation, you know, what are you going to do? You know, are you gonna-- where are you going to go to? Are you going to-- somebody tried to go to France. Somebody tried to go to this. Everybody tried. Or at least, you had these conversations. What, what do you think we can do? What can we do? We knew we were in a trap. Staying there was very dangerous. What did you know at that time about what was going around you? And did you have news from what was happening in Germany and to other Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were papers. It was mostly-- see, when-- once you were-- the Germans took over, then you had, of course, the German information only. But there was a black underground papers and news also that would reach you. Even the radio. We would, during the entire war, also later in France, listen to the underground radio. BBC mostly. They had-- they had programs for-- in English. And they had programs in German, and every language, practically. They were very well organized, very well done. And-- but it was very dangerous. Because when they came unexpected to, to check-- to, to walk in your house like the [NON-ENGLISH], the police, the first thing they would do, they go to the radio, turn on the knob. If you had it on a, on a British, on an Allied station, they took-- they arrested you right away. And it was very bad to turn it-- to turn it away. It was very hard to get because they had all these-- it was very, very difficult to get this. So when you had it, you'd like to keep it because it took a long time to get it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's-- that was one of the tricks they used, you know, to see if-- because it was against, against the law to listen to the BBC, or to Allied-- they had the too, which we listened to occasionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was usually two in the morning. It was a time change. So you had to get up at two in the morning to hear somebody speak, like Thomas Mann or whatever, would speak on the American radio for Germany, in German. And they had all kind of programs like that. So that's how you kept informed about what was really going on. We knew about concentration camps, for instance. And you didn't know every detail, but you knew. You knew what was happening. And then later, we knew, after D-day. You know, of course, we followed the Allied troops, every, every step of it. And we kept pretty well informed of the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're 12 years old in Antwerp during all this time. And when you had left, you were in school with Gentiles and Jews. When you went back, before you left to meet up with your father, what was your life like as a 12-year-old? And did you have any contact with these children anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, for a while I tried-- I went back to school, now that I think about it, for a while. And I remember, yes, but it was-- because they did, they did check schools also for Jewish children. So eventually, it became dangerous. For a while, I went back. And then-- and then Jewish children gradually, you know, dropped out of school because they were afraid. And, you know, the teachers or the principals, they could not-- not do anything for-- to protect them, even though they, they would have liked to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the same and France, you know? It was-- it was basically dangerous for Jewish children to go anywhere, in any public place. Because wherever you went, you never knew if they were going to check you out or something was going to happen. They would arrest a lot of kids in schools too. Did the Gentile children know you were Jewish at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We never kept it secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after German occupation, did you feel more anti-Semitism from the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. Not at all. I, I can't-- I can hardly remember any incident where I was a victim of anti-Semitism as a child. Really, no. It was-- it was very unusual. And even the teacher mentioned it officially. You know, the Jewish children would leave, and, and nobody would make a remark or anything. It was just accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came back and didn't register as, as Jews, were you with the Jewish children? Or did you separate at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not-- I think that's when I'd stopped going to school. Toward the end, I did not go to school. We preferred-- also, it didn't take us very long to leave. Let me think. We left-- we left-- yeah. We left about a year later for good, you know, for France, I think in April. I think. I'm not quite sure. It was like early spring the next year. And we prepared all this time. Because once we had contact with my father, that's when we decided, OK. We're going to go back to France. Because he urged us to. Like I said, now we-- when we were ready to leave, already, we had a place to leave our furniture and our belongings. And so when you had to prepare everything step by step. And then my, my mother had contacted, through friends, you know, this guy who specialized in smuggling people over the border to Vichy, France. That was a very, very well kept-- protected, defended border. They had-- they had Germans all along the border to avoid people passing, except with official papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had organized-- certain, certain people, for a lot of money, they'd organize ways to smuggle them-- to smuggle refugees over the border at night at certain spots. Maybe they had bribed some people there, some guards. I don't know how they-- how they managed to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother had managed to find-- that was, of course, the most decisive thing to do. To find a person who would be willing to smuggle us over that border for a lot of money. And so then finally, we decide, OK. We're going to go such and such day. We, we took a train, went to Montceau-les-Mines, which was the closest town near the border. And the man lived there in Montceau-les-Mines. We had his address. And we had paid him already. And my mother sent my brother to tell him that we were there. We were supposed to, to, to pass the border that same night. And then my brother comes back shortly after. And he says, bad news. He just got arrested by the Germans. The Germans just came and arrested him. And here we were like, in complete despair. We couldn't go back. We had broken off all the bridges. Besides, we couldn't go back where the Germans were. And we couldn't-- we could go over the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we were, trapped. We had no place to go. Not even, you know-- nothing. We're just standing there in the streets, looking at each other. What are we going to do? Where are you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montceau-les-Mines. In that little town in France. I'm sure it's still on the map somewhere. And my mother, out of despair, she looks around. She turns around, and she sees German headquarters. There was two guards, swastika flag flying in the wind. And she felt-- that's what she told me-- I've got nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she turns around. She walks up to the-- just without thinking. She walks up to the guard, and she says in German, what is the name of your commander in chief? And he was scared, because he was not allowed-- they're not allowed to talk. So he calls-- you know, side of his mouth. He calls somebody out. And another soldier comes out. And my mother repeats. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember his name. She says, I would like to see him. Is he available?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I'll see what I can do. And me and my-- and she tells my brother, you stay here. You know, It looks better if I have a girl. She takes me. I had my braids. I looked more presentable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walk in the German headquarters. And we have to wait. I don't know. But sure enough, the guy accepted us in. And he said, what, what is your name? Whatever-- it was the name of Cohn. Nowadays, I don't-- I don't quite understand how, you know, how that was possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- and finally, I remember, we walk in his office. It was this huge office. He was this German officer. It was all his medals, and his bright uniform, and his huge desk. And he stands up. My, my-- I have to say, my mother was good looking, and she was still young. And maybe that, that had something to do with it. And of course, our-- her German was fluent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he stands up and greets her, very polite. And so he said, what can I do for you? And my mother-- she says in two minutes she made up that story. So she tells him. Of course, she had to explain why she had the name Cohn. So she tells him that she was-- of course, she was from Germany, and she was married to this Cohn. And her husband was, was deported, which was the truth. And she had no idea where he was, which was not the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was desperate, because she was left without-- and she, she had planned to divorce him and we-- and start a new life, which, of course, was made up. And-- but she had a big problem, a financial problem, because she had two children to raise. And the only way she would do-- she has a relative who was well-to-do, and he lived in Vichy-- in Vichy France. And he had invited her and-- to bring-- to bring the children for the time being. And he would take care of us, completely. And she said it was the only-- it was her only-- her only salvation and the only way she could-- she could survive the war. But for this, she had to travel. She had to cross the demarcation line. And that was the only-- the only big problem. And for this, she would need a papers, you know, to cross the demarcation line.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he listened to her for quite a while. And I remember when my mother stopped speaking, he--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] --he looked at her, for like, a minute, without saying a word. And my mother said she knew perfectly well that he didn't-- he didn't buy it. He didn't buy a word of it, and he saw through her. And then he finally says, well, I'll see what I can do. But I want-- I want to warn you that as long as you stay here in the occupied territory, I could protect you. We can protect you. We can do things for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you cross the border, you realize that we are powerless? We have no influence anymore. And my mother says, yes, I know that. Of course, you know, that was the idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: He calls his, his, secretary or whatever, and he gives him instructions. And sure enough, he comes back a few minutes with the official German pass papers with the-- with the swastika on it, and the, the, the seal. The German seal, stamp, signatures, everything, for the name Cohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- and, and then he asked my mother-- and my mother thanks him. And he says, do you have a place? Where do you-- where are you staying? What, what place are you staying at? And my mother said, oh. We just arrived. I didn't have time to, to make reservations anywhere. We didn't have money to begin with. So he says, don't worry about it, I'll take care of it. You're going to go to such and such hotel and my-- and tell them I sent you. And we went to it. It was the nicest hotel in this-- in the town. Beautiful. Nothing but German officers. All German. They had taken over that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had paid for our dinner. That was the first decent meal we had in ages. And beautiful rooms and everything. Everything was paid for. And I mean, that's you know, another story that sounds incredible. And the next day, there was a bus. There was one bus a day. So we take the bus. And the bus takes us to-- maybe an hour or so, stops at the demarcation line. And here, two German guards come on the bus, you know, helmet, rifle, checking everybody's papers. The entire bus. And everybody was sitting there. You know, when the German-- there were German soldiers around officially, everybody was quiet. And everybody, you saw, you know, them turning pale in anticipation. You always expect something horrible to happen. And nobody said a word. And everybody was, you know, showing them the papers. And he comes to my mother. My mother hands him the paper without a word. He looks at this. He looks at her. He looks at the paper. He looks at her. Up and forth, up and forth. He was-- he didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he called the other guard over. He says, come-- come over here, I want to show you something. And he comes over. He says, look at this. What do you-- what do you make out of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, He sees Cohn with a seal and everything official. They had never seen this in their life. And I would-- I would be ready to bet this was the only German pass made out to a Jew, probably. And the other guy looked at it. He says, it's an order. It's official. There's nothing you can do. There's nothing wrong with it. And he gives it back to my mother, and he says, OK. And they leave the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said she, she, she was ready to faint, you know? Because again, she was thinking, I'm not going to make it. And the bus starts, and we were free. I mean, free. You know, relatively free. You were not free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was still a few hours a ride. And then all the way to Macon, which was in wine-- French wine country. And my father, meanwhile, had made friends with the mayor of Montceau-- it was a small town-- and the mayor-- and had told him his story. And the mayor said, if you manage to get your family come here, I'll take care of everything. They'll get papers. Because that was a problem too. You couldn't just go wherever you wanted, you know, and, and be accepted. They'll get papers to stay, and they get ration cards. You know, because that was part of it too, to get-- to get food. And everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we were, and we got our papers. But these were still papers with the name Cohn. So later-- you know, that's another story. The mayor of our village gave us papers with a fake name, in case we got checked out in the street. So they wouldn't arrest us. So you lived-- so now you met up with your father in Macon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Macon, yeah. And he lived in a little motel somewhere. It was like two small rooms. And we-- we lived there for quite awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not very fancy. Maybe-- we came there in spring. Till fall. Yeah, late fall. Maybe five, six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no German occupation in this particular area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No occupation. You didn't see any German uniforms. But the government was collaborating. They called it a [NON-ENGLISH]. They were collaborating with Germany, and they did everything they asked them to do. So the police worked for the-- you know, for the pro-German French government, which was the Vichy government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your father able, in that time, to live there? Did he work? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another-- that's another funny story, which sounds like-- you know, very unusual. After my father had left the camp, he was in Toulouse, all the way in the south, for a few days. And he walked the street, and here he sees somebody knows. And he says, Charles, what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it turns out he sees a French officer. And it was during the last days of the French-- before they were, they were taken over. And he was still wearing his uniform. And it was his first cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a first-- it was his first cousin. He was a banker in Paris. A very, very wealthy guy. Owned his own bank. And he died only in, in New York a few years ago. Maybe three years ago. A very fine man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, what are you doing? What are you doing? So my father tells him a story. And lo and behold, he was about to leave with his family to the United States, to save-- you know, to safety. But he left a trust fund for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lawyer whose name was Levy, and he had reversed his name during the war, made it-- turned it to Evelle. Every month we got a check from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people in Macon know that your father was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They knew. They knew very much so. But later, see, we moved from Macon. In fall, we moved from Macon. My father thought it was going to be safer to move to a, to a village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved to a village. He had made contact already with the mayor of that village. And so that's where we stayed during most of the war, in that village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here again, nobody ever asked us anything. Everybody knew we were Jewish. Everybody knew we were refugees who were running from the, from the Nazis. Nobody said a word. Nobody turned us in. It was like a well-kept secret. Officially, we were-- we were Catholics, and we went to Mass. But I'm sure they suspected, you know, what we were. And I went to a convent to school, because there was a private, private school for-- quite expensive, exclusive school, I would say. Mostly aristocratic. The children from aristocrats who went to school there, private school. All taught by nuns. [CLEARS THROAT] And my, my father went to talk to the Mother Superior. And she says, send your daughter here to school. You don't have to pay, you know, free of charge. And don't tell anybody you're Jewish. He told her. But she says, nobody should-- has to know. And so I went to-- I went to Mass and I went to confess. And I went, I did everything, just trying to figure out how to do it. And you know, trying to blend in. And I learned a lot about Catholicism. And so officially, we were Catholic, because, you know, that was the official religion there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who issued you-- you were talking the mayor of the town issued you your false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The mayor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your names-- was there anything special how the names got chosen? And what was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was, instead of Eva Cohn, became Jacqueline Moulin, which sounded very French. You know, I liked the name Jacqueline. And my first daughter, my first born, I named her Jacqueline in memory of this. Also, I think it's a nice name. And she never knew that, only after I showed her. Just recently, I showed her that, that fake identity card. She said, now I know why my name is Jacqueline. [LAUGHS] And he actually suggested it. It was his idea, the mayor. Because, he says, you know, it's not safe with the name Cohn. Because the situation became, gradually, more and more dangerous. At first it was a little more relaxed, but it became more and more dangerous. And we knew a group of Jews in Macon who lived in that area. And we would always meet with them, and we would always talk. You know, tragedy kind of draws you together. And they practically-- gradually, they were all arrested and disappeared. We were practically the only ones left when the war was over, of that group of Jews.
